Draft for discussion • Back office technology

A common capability taxonomy to unlock change capacity

A simple, shared language for Risk, Finance, Treasury and Controls CIOs — designed to compare across towers and benchmark vs peers, and to identify where AI + engineering practice changes can create real capacity.

Outcome: agreement to run the taxonomy exercise Focus: change, run and support
Back office CIO capability taxonomy • proposed structure
Slide 1
Context

Why this matters right now

The pressure is structural

  • Mandatory risk and compliance change continues to grow.
  • Run and resilience obligations remain non‑negotiable.
  • Delivery is shifting from waterfall toward tailored agile — while keeping strong controls.
AI
Tooling is moving from pilots to scale — but capacity only appears when operating practices change.
One
Shared taxonomy prevents local optimisation and enables meaningful peer comparison.
Key point: capacity is trapped in friction, handoffs and manual controls
Slide 2
Decision

Agree a common language — then target the biggest constraints

This is not an org redesign. It is a practical way to align CIOs on what capabilities exist, how mature they are, and where to invest to increase change throughput safely.

Step 1 — Define capabilities

Agree a cross‑CIO capability map (tool‑agnostic, benchmarkable).

Step 2 — Baseline maturity

Score the maturity and pain points consistently across towers.

Step 3 — Prioritise levers

Target the few moves that unlock the most capacity (incl. AI).

Call to action: approve the taxonomy exercise and workshops
Slide 3
What it is

A technology operating capability taxonomy

It describes how technology is delivered, operated and governed — independent of org design, tooling or location.

What it gives you

  • A common frame to compare Risk vs Finance vs Treasury vs Controls.
  • A basis to benchmark vs peers using observable signals.
  • A clean way to link AI adoption to measurable capacity outcomes.

What it is not

  • Not a SAFe implementation plan (it complements tailored agile).
  • Not a tool catalogue (tooling maps later).
  • Not a re‑org proposal (decision rights come after baseline).
Design intent: simple enough for CIO alignment, detailed enough to act on
Slide 4
Proposed structure

Level 1 capability groups

Twelve groups cover the full change‑run‑control lifecycle and work across all back office CIO towers.

Plan & govern

Strategy, governance & financial management Demand, product & delivery management Architecture & engineering design

Build & release

Software engineering build Quality engineering Release, environment & change enablement

Run & assure

Service management & operations Reliability, observability & resilience Security, risk & controls in technology

Enable at scale

Data, reporting & data controls Platform & infrastructure services AI enablement & governance People, culture & ways of working
Next: confirm definitions, boundaries and owners per group
Slide 5
Insight

Capacity comes from reducing friction and rework

AI helps — but only when paired with standardised engineering practices, automation, and control-by-design.

Friction in delivery

Handoffs, approvals, unclear requirements and dependency queues slow throughput.

Rework in quality

Manual testing, unstable environments and late defects consume capacity twice.

Manual controls burden

Evidence creation and audit support is essential — but can be automated.

Operational toil

Repetitive incidents and ticket churn drain change capacity.

Data delays

Data access, lineage and quality issues block delivery and reporting.

Toolchain fragmentation

Multiple ways of working make scale hard across onshore/offshore/captives.

Use the taxonomy to: pinpoint where standardisation pays back fastest
Slide 6
AI overlay

Where AI is most likely to create near-term capacity

Start with assistive use cases in the SDLC and operations — then scale with governance and measurement.

High impact (12 months)

  • Engineering productivity: code, tests, documentation and knowledge search.
  • Quality acceleration: test generation, coverage insights, defect triage support.
  • Operations assist: incident triage summaries, runbook suggestions, alert noise reduction.

Scale safely (governance)

  • Approved tools, data boundaries, and secure deployment patterns.
  • Monitoring and evaluation: accuracy, drift, auditability, and human-in-the-loop controls.
  • Evidence-by-design: automate control evidence capture in the delivery flow.
Message for stakeholders: we prioritise safety and measurable benefits
Slide 7
Delivery reality

Designed for hybrid sourcing and captives

The taxonomy separates capabilities from where work happens, enabling consistent delivery across internal, SI, offshore and nearshore teams.

Standardise the “how”

Common engineering standards, quality gates, environments, and evidence patterns.

Keep decision rights clear

Define what is enterprise‑standard vs tower‑local vs platform‑owned.

Make work portable

Reusable patterns enable smooth movement of work across locations and partners.

Build capability, not dependency

Use partners to accelerate maturity while growing internal and captive depth.

Measure consistently

Flow, quality, resilience and controls metrics are comparable across towers.

Protect licence to operate

Controls remain non‑negotiable — the goal is to automate and embed them.

Key point: hybrid sourcing works best with shared standards and platforms
Slide 8
Approach

A short, practical exercise with clear outputs

We keep it lightweight: agree definitions, baseline maturity, and identify the few moves that release the most capacity.

Workshops (2–3 sessions)

  • Confirm L1/L2 capability definitions and boundaries.
  • Identify shared capabilities to standardise and platformise.
  • Pressure-test using real initiatives (regulatory, resilience, AI).

Outputs

  • Capability map + definitions (the “dictionary”).
  • Cross‑CIO maturity snapshot and key constraints.
  • Prioritised roadmap of capacity levers (12/36/60 months).
Principle: one clear message per slide, with action at the end
Slide 9
Next step

Confirm sponsors and schedule the taxonomy workshops

Once we agree the common language, we can compare across CIO towers, benchmark externally, and focus AI + engineering change on the few areas that unlock the most capacity.

Participants

CIO reps + architecture + security + controls + ops + data governance.

Inputs

Existing SDLC/ITSM standards, tooling view, audit themes, portfolio mix.

Decision

Agree the taxonomy and the shortlist of capacity levers to pursue first.

Draft for discussion • use as an opening conversation pack
Slide 10
